title: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis
oneLiner: Progressive, irreversible scarring of lung interstitium of unknown cause, typically in older adults (60+), leading to respiratory failure and death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute exacerbation of IPF:** Sudden worsening of dyspnoea (<30 days) with new bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT – NOT infection. Key finding: rapid oxygen desaturation ± respiratory acidosis despite standard therapy.
- **Pulmonary embolism (PE):** Pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, unilateral leg swelling, tachycardia disproportionate to hypoxia. Single finding: raised D-dimer (but may be non-specific in ILD) + CT pulmonary angiogram confirms.
- **Pneumothorax:** Sudden pleuritic pain + tracheal deviation/hyper-resonance + absent breath sounds. **Do not needle decompress unless tension – urgent chest drain.**
- **Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP):** Fever, purulent sputum, raised CRP/procalcitonin. Key differentiator: sputum culture & response to antibiotics – if no improvement in 48h, consider acute exacerbation.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive exertional dyspnoea (over months–years)
  - Dry, non-productive cough (often relentless)
  - Bilateral “Velcro” crackles on auscultation (fine, end-inspiratory)
  - Finger clubbing (in ~40–60%)
  - No significant extra-pulmonary features (e.g. no rash, joint pain, uveitis – those point to connective tissue disease)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Basal fine crackles (never clear with cough)
  - Clubbing
  - Signs of right heart strain (if pulmonary hypertension develops): raised JVP, heave, loud P2, peripheral oedema
  - Resting hypoxia ± 6-minute walk test desaturation
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP):** History of bird/feather/mould exposure; HRCT shows upper zone fibrosis ± mosaic attenuation; BAL lymphocytosis >20%; specific IgG antibodies.
- **Connective tissue disease-associated ILD (CTD-ILD):** Extra-pulmonary features (Raynaud’s, arthritis, rash, myositis); positive ANA, RF, anti-CCP, anti-synthetase antibodies; HRCT may show NSIP pattern (bibasal, symmetric, ground-glass sparing subpleural).
- **Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis:** Same as above, but absence of precipitating factors; sometimes need BAL and lung biopsy.
- **Sarcoidosis:** Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, uveitis, erythema nodosum; HRCT shows perilymphatic nodules, upper lobe predominance; serum ACE/raised calcium.
- **Drug-induced ILD:** Chemotherapy (bleomycin, methotrexate), amiodarone, nitrofurantoin, biologics – temporal relationship.
- **Asbestosis:** Pleural plaques, prior occupational exposure; HRCT shows subpleural lines, parenchymal bands.
- **Splitter:** The single most useful test to differentiate IPF from all other ILDs is **HRCT chest** – a “definite UIP” pattern (honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, basal-predominant, subpleural) is virtually diagnostic of IPF. Any atypical features (upper lobe, mosaic attenuation, prominent ground-glass, fibrotic in upper lobes) mandate multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion ± biopsy.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - SpO₂ → ABG if <92% on room air or acute deterioration
  - 6-minute walk test (if stable) – desaturation >4% or <88% indicates need for oxygen
  - ECG: look for right axis deviation, P pulmonale, RV hypertrophy
  - Urine pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age before high-dose steroids or antifibrotic therapy)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, coagulation
  - Autoimmune screen: ANA, RF, anti-CCP, anti-Jo1 (anti-synthetase syndrome), anti-Scl70 (systemic sclerosis), anti-Ro/La (Sjögren’s)
  - Hypersensitivity pneumonitis panel: bird serum precipitins, mould-specific IgG
  - ACE (if sarcoid suspected)
  - BNP/NT-proBNP (if right heart strain)
  - D-dimer (if PE suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **HRCT chest** (thin-section, supine + prone inspiratory and expiratory) – definitive diagnosis in ~70%
  - Second-line: CTPA (if acute deterioration to rule out PE or confirm acute exacerbation)
- **Special tests:**
  - Pulmonary function tests (PFTs): FVC, FEV1, TLC, DLCO – restrictive pattern, reduced DLCO (<80% predicted is typical; <40% predicts poor outcome)
  - 6-minute walk test
  - Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) – if atypical features (lymphocytes >30% suggests HP; eosinophils >5% suggests eosinophilic pneumonia)
  - Lung biopsy (video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery – VATS) – for non-definite UIP pattern; only if MDT unclear and patient fit enough
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE guideline: diagnosis requires HRCT showing definite UIP pattern **OR** probable/indeterminate UIP with histopathology showing UIP (via lung biopsy) **after** exclusion of other causes (MDT discussion)
  - **Never-miss test:** **HRCT chest** – failure to do this delays diagnosis and may miss alternative treatable ILD (e.g. HP). Also, **pregnancy test** before prescribing pirfenidone/nintedanib (teratogenic).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Oxygen to target SpO₂ 88–92% (if type 2 respiratory failure risk) or 94–98% if acute deterioration (check ABG)
  - Treat underlying trigger if acute exacerbation: **avoid high-flow O₂ without CO₂ monitoring**; consider non-invasive ventilation (NIV) if type 2 failure; high-dose corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 500 mg–1 g IV daily for 3 days – **check local policy**; no strong evidence but commonly used)
  - If PE suspected: start LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg daily) pending CTPA
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Start antifibrotic therapy: **pirfenidone or nintedanib** – only if definite IPF and after discussion with respiratory specialist/MDT
  - Acute exacerbation: continue steroids – methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily for 3 days, then oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day tapered over weeks (**check local policy**)
  - Monitor oxygen requirements, daily SpO₂, ABG if deteriorating
  - Chest physiotherapy for cough management
  - Palliate symptoms: antitussives (e.g. simple linctus, morphine for cough if severe)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Antifibrotic therapy indefinitely (if tolerated)
  - Pulmonary rehabilitation (improves exercise capacity, QoL)
  - Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) – assess for home oxygen if SpO₂ <88% at rest or desaturates <88% on exercise
  - Lung transplantation assessment (if <65–70 years, no major comorbidities)
  - Vaccinations: annual influenza, one-off pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23)
  - Smoking cessation counselling
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 20–30 mL/hour (avoid excess – risk of pulmonary oedema if right heart failure)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg daily SC) unless contraindicated (bleeding, creatinine clearance <30 – use unfractionated heparin)
  - **Diet:** normal diet unless NBM for biopsy; encourage high-calorie, high-protein if weight loss
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 q4h (or q1h if unstable); SpO₂ 88–92% target; monitor for signs of acute exacerbation
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line antifibrotic:**
  - **Pirfenidone:** Start 267 mg TID PO, titrate up over 2 weeks to 801 mg TID (267 mg × 3 capsules TID). Check LFTs at baseline, then monthly for 6 months. Reduce dose if GI intolerance or hepatotoxicity. [Check local policy for dose adjustment protocol.]
  - **Nintedanib:** 150 mg BD PO with food. Reduce to 100 mg BD if side effects (diarrhoea, nausea, LFT elevation). Contraindicated if severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C). Check LFTs at baseline, then monthly for 3 months, then q3monthly.
- **Acute exacerbation:**
  - **Methylprednisolone IV:** 500 mg–1 g daily for 3 days (check local protocol). Then oral prednisolone taper (0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks, then taper over 4–8 weeks). – **No strong evidence; use only after MDT discussion.**
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **N-acetylcysteine** (no benefit shown, no longer recommended)
  - **Antacids** – empirical use of PPI (e.g. omeprazole 20 mg OD) for gastro-oesophageal reflux which may exacerbate cough – weak evidence, but common practice.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Pirfenidone: potent CYP1A2 inhibitors (fluvoxamine, ciprofloxacin) – increase exposure; avoid grapefruit juice.
  - Nintedanib: P-glycoprotein inhibitors (e.g. verapamil, amiodarone); caution with anticoagulants (increased bleeding risk).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Pirfenidone: contraindicated if severe hepatic impairment; moderate hepatic impairment – reduce dose to 267 mg TID (check local policy). Renal: no dose adjustment needed, but no data in severe renal impairment (eGFR <30).
  - Nintedanib: mild–moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B) – reduce to 100 mg BD; severe (C) – not recommended. Renal: no dose adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Antifibrotic overdose is unlikely to cause immediate harm, but missing LFT monitoring can lead to severe hepatotoxicity.** **Also, acute exacerbation: giving high-dose methylprednisolone without checking for active infection (e.g. sputum cultures) can worsen outcomes.** **Do not prescribe antifibrotic drugs until the diagnosis is confirmed by MDT – prescribing in misdiagnosed HP or CTD-ILD can be harmful.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Acute exacerbation of IPF (life-threatening, mortality ~50%)
  - Pulmonary embolism (risk increased in chronic lung disease)
  - Spontaneous pneumothorax (if subpleural blebs)
  - Respiratory failure (type 1 or 2)
  - Adverse drug reactions (pirfenidone: photosensitivity, GI upset, LFT elevation; nintedanib: diarrhoea, nausea, LFT elevation)
- **Chronic:**
  - Pulmonary hypertension ± cor pulmonale
  - Chronic respiratory failure → need for LTOT
  - Progressive weight loss (cachexia)
  - Lung cancer (increased risk, especially in fibrotic areas)
sections.Prognosis: - Median survival 3–5 years from diagnosis (untreated). Antifibrotic therapy slows decline but does not halt progression. Predictors of worse prognosis: baseline FVC <80% predicted, DLCO <40% predicted, desaturation on 6-min walk, acute exacerbation. Lung transplantation offers survival benefit (5-year survival ~50%).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do a thorough occupational/environmental history** when first suspecting IPF – many cases of HP are missed because no one asks about birds, feathers, mouldy hay, hot tubs. This directs treatment (avoidance) and may be curative.
- **Always obtain a 6-minute walk test** at baseline and follow-up – desaturation pattern is a strong predictor of outcomes and guides oxygen therapy.
- **Ensure the patient is enrolled in pulmonary rehabilitation** – it improves dyspnoea, exercise capacity, and QoL, and reduces hospital admissions.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not diagnose IPF without HRCT reviewed by an ILD MDT** – up to 30% of cases of IPF-like fibrosis are actually HP or CTD-ILD. Mistreating these with antifibrotic alone (instead of immunosuppression or antigen avoidance) worsens outcomes.
- **Do not give high-dose steroids for acute exacerbation without first ruling out infection** – send sputum cultures, blood cultures, and consider bronchoalveolar lavage before starting corticosteroids.
- **Do not start antifibrotic therapy in patients with advanced disease (FVC <50% predicted) or severe comorbidities** – benefit is minimal and side effects are high. Discuss with respiratory specialist.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening breathlessness (increased by >1 point on MRC scale over days), new haemoptysis, fever, chest pain, or inability to speak in full sentences.
- **Return if:** side effects from medication (severe nausea, diarrhoea, jaundice, photosensitivity rash) – do not stop antifibrotic without speaking to specialist.
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient respiratory/ILD clinic within 4 weeks of discharge. Pulmonary rehabilitation referral. Spirometry + DLCO at 3–6 months. Annual influenza vaccination.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call respiratory specialist (SpR/consultant) if:** acute worsening with NEW2 ≥5, SpO₂ <88% despite high-flow oxygen, respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35 + pCO₂ >6.5), new signs of right heart failure (JVP raised, ankle oedema, syncope).
- **Call ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥7, impending intubation (GCS <8, exhaustion, pH <7.25), non-invasive ventilation fails (no improvement after 30–60 min), shock (MAP <65 mmHg, lactate >4 mmol/L).
- **Specific thresholds for acute exacerbation:** PaO₂ <8 kPa on oxygen, rapid decline in FVC >10% over 1 month, need for mechanical ventilation – consider ICU referral early, but prognosis is poor (mortality >80–90% if ventilated).

rawMarkdown:
## Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis

**One-liner:** Progressive, irreversible scarring of lung interstitium of unknown cause, typically in older adults (60+), leading to respiratory failure and death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute exacerbation of IPF:** Sudden worsening of dyspnoea (<30 days) with new bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT – NOT infection. Key finding: rapid oxygen desaturation ± respiratory acidosis despite standard therapy.
- **Pulmonary embolism (PE):** Pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, unilateral leg swelling, tachycardia disproportionate to hypoxia. Single finding: raised D-dimer (but may be non-specific in ILD) + CT pulmonary angiogram confirms.
- **Pneumothorax:** Sudden pleuritic pain + tracheal deviation/hyper-resonance + absent breath sounds. **Do not needle decompress unless tension – urgent chest drain.**
- **Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP):** Fever, purulent sputum, raised CRP/procalcitonin. Key differentiator: sputum culture & response to antibiotics – if no improvement in 48h, consider acute exacerbation.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive exertional dyspnoea (over months–years)
  - Dry, non-productive cough (often relentless)
  - Bilateral “Velcro” crackles on auscultation (fine, end-inspiratory)
  - Finger clubbing (in ~40–60%)
  - No significant extra-pulmonary features (e.g. no rash, joint pain, uveitis – those point to connective tissue disease)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Basal fine crackles (never clear with cough)
  - Clubbing
  - Signs of right heart strain (if pulmonary hypertension develops): raised JVP, heave, loud P2, peripheral oedema
  - Resting hypoxia ± 6-minute walk test desaturation

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP):** History of bird/feather/mould exposure; HRCT shows upper zone fibrosis ± mosaic attenuation; BAL lymphocytosis >20%; specific IgG antibodies.
- **Connective tissue disease-associated ILD (CTD-ILD):** Extra-pulmonary features (Raynaud’s, arthritis, rash, myositis); positive ANA, RF, anti-CCP, anti-synthetase antibodies; HRCT may show NSIP pattern (bibasal, symmetric, ground-glass sparing subpleural).
- **Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis:** Same as above, but absence of precipitating factors; sometimes need BAL and lung biopsy.
- **Sarcoidosis:** Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, uveitis, erythema nodosum; HRCT shows perilymphatic nodules, upper lobe predominance; serum ACE/raised calcium.
- **Drug-induced ILD:** Chemotherapy (bleomycin, methotrexate), amiodarone, nitrofurantoin, biologics – temporal relationship.
- **Asbestosis:** Pleural plaques, prior occupational exposure; HRCT shows subpleural lines, parenchymal bands.
- **Splitter:** The single most useful test to differentiate IPF from all other ILDs is **HRCT chest** – a “definite UIP” pattern (honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, basal-predominant, subpleural) is virtually diagnostic of IPF. Any atypical features (upper lobe, mosaic attenuation, prominent ground-glass, fibrotic in upper lobes) mandate multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion ± biopsy.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - SpO₂ → ABG if <92% on room air or acute deterioration
  - 6-minute walk test (if stable) – desaturation >4% or <88% indicates need for oxygen
  - ECG: look for right axis deviation, P pulmonale, RV hypertrophy
  - Urine pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age before high-dose steroids or antifibrotic therapy)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, coagulation
  - Autoimmune screen: ANA, RF, anti-CCP, anti-Jo1 (anti-synthetase syndrome), anti-Scl70 (systemic sclerosis), anti-Ro/La (Sjögren’s)
  - Hypersensitivity pneumonitis panel: bird serum precipitins, mould-specific IgG
  - ACE (if sarcoid suspected)
  - BNP/NT-proBNP (if right heart strain)
  - D-dimer (if PE suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **HRCT chest** (thin-section, supine + prone inspiratory and expiratory) – definitive diagnosis in ~70%
  - Second-line: CTPA (if acute deterioration to rule out PE or confirm acute exacerbation)
- **Special tests:**
  - Pulmonary function tests (PFTs): FVC, FEV1, TLC, DLCO – restrictive pattern, reduced DLCO (<80% predicted is typical; <40% predicts poor outcome)
  - 6-minute walk test
  - Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) – if atypical features (lymphocytes >30% suggests HP; eosinophils >5% suggests eosinophilic pneumonia)
  - Lung biopsy (video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery – VATS) – for non-definite UIP pattern; only if MDT unclear and patient fit enough
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE guideline: diagnosis requires HRCT showing definite UIP pattern **OR** probable/indeterminate UIP with histopathology showing UIP (via lung biopsy) **after** exclusion of other causes (MDT discussion)
  - **Never-miss test:** **HRCT chest** – failure to do this delays diagnosis and may miss alternative treatable ILD (e.g. HP). Also, **pregnancy test** before prescribing pirfenidone/nintedanib (teratogenic).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Oxygen to target SpO₂ 88–92% (if type 2 respiratory failure risk) or 94–98% if acute deterioration (check ABG)
  - Treat underlying trigger if acute exacerbation: **avoid high-flow O₂ without CO₂ monitoring**; consider non-invasive ventilation (NIV) if type 2 failure; high-dose corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 500 mg–1 g IV daily for 3 days – **check local policy**; no strong evidence but commonly used)
  - If PE suspected: start LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg daily) pending CTPA
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Start antifibrotic therapy: **pirfenidone or nintedanib** – only if definite IPF and after discussion with respiratory specialist/MDT
  - Acute exacerbation: continue steroids – methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily for 3 days, then oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day tapered over weeks (**check local policy**)
  - Monitor oxygen requirements, daily SpO₂, ABG if deteriorating
  - Chest physiotherapy for cough management
  - Palliate symptoms: antitussives (e.g. simple linctus, morphine for cough if severe)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Antifibrotic therapy indefinitely (if tolerated)
  - Pulmonary rehabilitation (improves exercise capacity, QoL)
  - Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) – assess for home oxygen if SpO₂ <88% at rest or desaturates <88% on exercise
  - Lung transplantation assessment (if <65–70 years, no major comorbidities)
  - Vaccinations: annual influenza, one-off pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23)
  - Smoking cessation counselling
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 20–30 mL/hour (avoid excess – risk of pulmonary oedema if right heart failure)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg daily SC) unless contraindicated (bleeding, creatinine clearance <30 – use unfractionated heparin)
  - **Diet:** normal diet unless NBM for biopsy; encourage high-calorie, high-protein if weight loss
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 q4h (or q1h if unstable); SpO₂ 88–92% target; monitor for signs of acute exacerbation

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line antifibrotic:**
  - **Pirfenidone:** Start 267 mg TID PO, titrate up over 2 weeks to 801 mg TID (267 mg × 3 capsules TID). Check LFTs at baseline, then monthly for 6 months. Reduce dose if GI intolerance or hepatotoxicity. [Check local policy for dose adjustment protocol.]
  - **Nintedanib:** 150 mg BD PO with food. Reduce to 100 mg BD if side effects (diarrhoea, nausea, LFT elevation). Contraindicated if severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C). Check LFTs at baseline, then monthly for 3 months, then q3monthly.
- **Acute exacerbation:**
  - **Methylprednisolone IV:** 500 mg–1 g daily for 3 days (check local protocol). Then oral prednisolone taper (0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks, then taper over 4–8 weeks). – **No strong evidence; use only after MDT discussion.**
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **N-acetylcysteine** (no benefit shown, no longer recommended)
  - **Antacids** – empirical use of PPI (e.g. omeprazole 20 mg OD) for gastro-oesophageal reflux which may exacerbate cough – weak evidence, but common practice.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Pirfenidone: potent CYP1A2 inhibitors (fluvoxamine, ciprofloxacin) – increase exposure; avoid grapefruit juice.
  - Nintedanib: P-glycoprotein inhibitors (e.g. verapamil, amiodarone); caution with anticoagulants (increased bleeding risk).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Pirfenidone: contraindicated if severe hepatic impairment; moderate hepatic impairment – reduce dose to 267 mg TID (check local policy). Renal: no dose adjustment needed, but no data in severe renal impairment (eGFR <30).
  - Nintedanib: mild–moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B) – reduce to 100 mg BD; severe (C) – not recommended. Renal: no dose adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Antifibrotic overdose is unlikely to cause immediate harm, but missing LFT monitoring can lead to severe hepatotoxicity.** **Also, acute exacerbation: giving high-dose methylprednisolone without checking for active infection (e.g. sputum cultures) can worsen outcomes.** **Do not prescribe antifibrotic drugs until the diagnosis is confirmed by MDT – prescribing in misdiagnosed HP or CTD-ILD can be harmful.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Acute exacerbation of IPF (life-threatening, mortality ~50%)
  - Pulmonary embolism (risk increased in chronic lung disease)
  - Spontaneous pneumothorax (if subpleural blebs)
  - Respiratory failure (type 1 or 2)
  - Adverse drug reactions (pirfenidone: photosensitivity, GI upset, LFT elevation; nintedanib: diarrhoea, nausea, LFT elevation)
- **Chronic:**
  - Pulmonary hypertension ± cor pulmonale
  - Chronic respiratory failure → need for LTOT
  - Progressive weight loss (cachexia)
  - Lung cancer (increased risk, especially in fibrotic areas)

### Prognosis
- Median survival 3–5 years from diagnosis (untreated). Antifibrotic therapy slows decline but does not halt progression. Predictors of worse prognosis: baseline FVC <80% predicted, DLCO <40% predicted, desaturation on 6-min walk, acute exacerbation. Lung transplantation offers survival benefit (5-year survival ~50%).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do a thorough occupational/environmental history** when first suspecting IPF – many cases of HP are missed because no one asks about birds, feathers, mouldy hay, hot tubs. This directs treatment (avoidance) and may be curative.
- **Always obtain a 6-minute walk test** at baseline and follow-up – desaturation pattern is a strong predictor of outcomes and guides oxygen therapy.
- **Ensure the patient is enrolled in pulmonary rehabilitation** – it improves dyspnoea, exercise capacity, and QoL, and reduces hospital admissions.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not diagnose IPF without HRCT reviewed by an ILD MDT** – up to 30% of cases of IPF-like fibrosis are actually HP or CTD-ILD. Mistreating these with antifibrotic alone (instead of immunosuppression or antigen avoidance) worsens outcomes.
- **Do not give high-dose steroids for acute exacerbation without first ruling out infection** – send sputum cultures, blood cultures, and consider bronchoalveolar lavage before starting corticosteroids.
- **Do not start antifibrotic therapy in patients with advanced disease (FVC <50% predicted) or severe comorbidities** – benefit is minimal and side effects are high. Discuss with respiratory specialist.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening breathlessness (increased by >1 point on MRC scale over days), new haemoptysis, fever, chest pain, or inability to speak in full sentences.
- **Return if:** side effects from medication (severe nausea, diarrhoea, jaundice, photosensitivity rash) – do not stop antifibrotic without speaking to specialist.
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient respiratory/ILD clinic within 4 weeks of discharge. Pulmonary rehabilitation referral. Spirometry + DLCO at 3–6 months. Annual influenza vaccination.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call respiratory specialist (SpR/consultant) if:** acute worsening with NEW2 ≥5, SpO₂ <88% despite high-flow oxygen, respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35 + pCO₂ >6.5), new signs of right heart failure (JVP raised, ankle oedema, syncope).
- **Call ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥7, impending intubation (GCS <8, exhaustion, pH <7.25), non-invasive ventilation fails (no improvement after 30–60 min), shock (MAP <65 mmHg, lactate >4 mmol/L).
- **Specific thresholds for acute exacerbation:** PaO₂ <8 kPa on oxygen, rapid decline in FVC >10% over 1 month, need for mechanical ventilation – consider ICU referral early, but prognosis is poor (mortality >80–90% if ventilated).
